title: Bengaluru Heatwave: Viral Video Shows Crayons Melting, Leaves Internet Shocked | WATCH - Asianet Newsable
full_text:
A content creator posted a video purporting to show crayons melting in the sun, casting doubt on Bengaluru's reputation for nice weather. She said how shocked she was by the unheard-of heat in the city and wondered if it was hot enough to fry an egg outside. A broader discussion on climate change and the quickly evolving environment of India's garden city has been spurred by the video.
“Guys first time experiencing this kind of hotness! Do you think I should fry an omelette on my table?” digital content creator Geetha Shree Nagaraaj wrote. "You won't believe Bengaluru is getting so hot!" is the text insert that appears at the beginning of the video. Then it displays her startled response.
In the scene that follows, she records a tray full of crayons that seem to be melting to illustrate what she means by her remark. "Never in my entire life in Bangalore has this happened," she declares.
"OMG, the crayons are melting," she exclaims as she rattles the tray. She asks whether she might fry an egg with the heat as the video comes to a close.
"Let's all start growing trees in front of our house or on the terrace to balance this," someone proposed. "Yes, true," Nagaraaj said.
Another expressed, “Yet Bangalore is the only city where people can dare to step out in the hot sun. Can you imagine in any other city? Delhi? Chennai?? Mumbai??” The content creator responded, “Oh, no way. Yes, yes, even in this heat it’s still possible to step out !”
“You guys waste so much water in the name of getting RO water instead of recycling it. Prepare yourself for the severe water shortage that will follow,” wrote a third person
"Waiting for the omelette reel" was written by a fourth. "I think I made a rash decision; it'll ruin my IKEA table," Nagaraaj retorted.
Karnataka is experiencing a severe heatwave, not only Bengaluru. Rainfall is predicted for today, April 18, according to the India Meteorological Department (IMD). Bengaluru has had more hot and muggy summers over the last ten years, and 2026 is no different. This tendency is attributed by experts to a number of factors, including growing urbanisation and declining open space.

title: Bengaluru Heatwave Alert: Ten Suspected Heatstroke Cases Reported; Health Department Warns - Asianet Newsable
full_text:
Once celebrated as the ‘Garden City’ and ‘Air-Conditioned City’ for its pleasant climate, Bengaluru is now grappling with a sharp rise in temperatures. The city is witnessing unusually hot conditions this summer, raising growing concerns among residents and authorities alike. In a worrying development, the city’s health department has confirmed that at least 10 people have fallen ill due to suspected heatstroke, signalling the early impact of the ongoing heatwave.
Between March 1 and April 16, a total of 10 suspected heatstroke cases were reported from different parts of the city. All affected individuals were treated at corporation-run hospitals. Officials noted that this is the first time in recent years such a number of cases has been recorded, and each incident is being treated seriously as a potential heat-related illness.
In response, the Bruhat Bengaluru Mahanagara Palike (BBMP) has gone on high alert. Health officials have directed ASHA workers and other frontline staff to actively spread awareness among residents on precautions to stay safe during extreme heat conditions.
Dr Siraj Madani, Chief Health Officer, stated that the department is closely monitoring the situation. Health inspectors at Primary Health Centres across all 369 wards have been instructed to remain available during peak afternoon hours to attend to individuals reporting heat-related symptoms.
He added that most affected individuals were found to be severely dehydrated. In some cases, people fell ill after spending extended periods inside parked vehicles, where temperatures can rise rapidly to dangerous levels.
Residents have been advised to seek immediate medical attention if they experience symptoms such as dizziness, fatigue, or blurred vision. Early intervention can help prevent complications associated with heatstroke.
To increase public awareness, informational posters have been displayed at 143 Primary Health Centres and 242 Namma Clinics. These posters encourage residents to stay hydrated and recommend consuming tender coconut water, buttermilk, and other cooling fluids.

Authorities continue to monitor the situation closely and have urged residents to remain cautious, as temperatures are expected to stay high in the coming days.
